questions.question_text: Michigan's Elliott-Larsen Civil Rights Act protects which classes that federal fair housing law does NOT cover?
questions.correct_answer: Age, marital status, and sexual orientation
distractors: Only race and national origin are added
Religion and disability protections only
Familial status and color protections only
questions.explanation: The Elliott-Larsen Civil Rights Act goes beyond the classes enumerated in the federal Fair Housing Act by protecting age, marital status, sexual orientation, and gender identity or expression in housing (MCL 37.2502). Effective April 2, 2025, it also protects source of income in rental housing (2024 PA 180). None of these classes is enumerated in federal fair housing law.

questions.question_text: A Michigan landlord who owns a 20-unit apartment building refuses to rent to a couple because they are unmarried. Is this lawful?
questions.correct_answer: No, marital status is protected in Michigan
distractors: Yes, landlords may set moral standards
Yes, unless the property has 5+ units
Only if the landlord lives on the premises
questions.explanation: Michigan's Elliott-Larsen Civil Rights Act protects marital status in housing (MCL 37.2502), so refusing to rent because tenants are unmarried is unlawful discrimination. A narrow exemption exists only for owner-occupied buildings of two units or fewer and rooms rented in the lessor's own residence (MCL 37.2503), which does not apply to a 20-unit building.

questions.question_text: A 62-year-old applies to rent an apartment in Michigan but is denied because the landlord prefers younger tenants. What law has the landlord violated?
distractors: The federal Fair Housing Act only
No law, age is not protected in housing
Michigan's Senior Housing Act
questions.explanation: Age is a protected class under Michigan's Elliott-Larsen Civil Rights Act for housing. Unlike federal law, which does not protect age in housing, Michigan explicitly prohibits this form of discrimination.

questions.question_text: A Michigan real estate agent steers a same-sex couple away from a certain neighborhood. Under which state law can the couple file a complaint?
distractors: Michigan Occupational Code only
Michigan Consumer Protection Act
No state law covers this situation
questions.explanation: Sexual orientation is a protected class under Michigan's Elliott-Larsen Civil Rights Act. The Michigan Supreme Court's Rouch World decision (2022) recognized sexual-orientation discrimination as sex discrimination, and PA 6 of 2023 wrote sexual orientation and gender identity or expression into the statute effective February 13, 2024. Steering based on sexual orientation violates this law.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is a protected class under BOTH federal fair housing law AND Michigan's Elliott-Larsen Act?
distractors: Marital status
Age
Sexual orientation
questions.explanation: Familial status is protected under both the federal Fair Housing Act and Michigan's Elliott-Larsen Act. Marital status, age, and sexual orientation are Michigan-only protections not covered by federal housing law.

questions.question_text: A tenant with a mobility disability requests a ramp at the entrance of her Michigan apartment. The landlord refuses, citing building aesthetics. Is this lawful?
questions.correct_answer: No, reasonable modifications must be allowed
distractors: Yes, landlords control the building appearance
Only if the tenant pays for full restoration
Only in buildings with 4 or more rental units
questions.explanation: Under Michigan's Persons with Disabilities Civil Rights Act (1976 PA 220), a housing provider may not refuse to permit reasonable modifications made at the expense of the person with a disability (MCL 37.1506a). For rentals, the landlord may condition permission on restoring the interior, reasonable wear and tear excepted, but cannot refuse based on aesthetics. Note that disability is protected by the PDCRA and federal law, not by Elliott-Larsen.

questions.question_text: A Michigan homeowner with a visual impairment requests a waiver of the 'no pets' policy for her guide dog. What must the HOA do?
questions.correct_answer: Grant the request as a reasonable accommodation
distractors: Deny it because rules apply to all owners
Allow it only with a standard pet deposit
Require a vote of all current HOA members
questions.explanation: Waiving a no-pets policy for a guide dog is a reasonable accommodation required under Michigan's Persons with Disabilities Civil Rights Act (MCL 37.1506a) and the federal Fair Housing Act. Because an assistance animal is not a pet, HUD guidance and the accommodation duty bar charging pet deposits or pet fees for it. Denying the waiver or conditioning it on a deposit is unlawful.

questions.question_text: Where does a person file a housing discrimination complaint under Michigan state law?
distractors: Michigan Real Estate Commission
Local circuit court only
Michigan Attorney General's office
questions.explanation: Housing discrimination complaints under Michigan law are filed with the Michigan Department of Civil Rights (MDCR), which investigates and can pursue remedies including cease and desist orders and damages.

questions.question_text: Which remedies can the MDCR order in a Michigan housing discrimination case?
questions.correct_answer: Cease and desist plus actual damages
distractors: Criminal imprisonment penalties only
Revocation of the real estate license only
A formal written apology letter only
questions.explanation: After the MDCR investigates, the Michigan Civil Rights Commission may order the discriminatory practice to cease and desist and award actual damages, including attorney fees (MCL 37.2605). In real estate cases it may also impose civil fines of up to $10,000 for a first violation, $25,000 for a second within 5 years, and $50,000 for two or more within 7 years. Private lawsuits remain available as an alternative path (MCL 37.2801).

questions.question_text: A Michigan buyer believes she was denied a mortgage because of her disability. Besides filing with MDCR, what other option does she have?
questions.correct_answer: File a private lawsuit in court
distractors: She must use MDCR exclusively
Appeal to the Michigan Real Estate Commission
File a police report for discrimination
questions.explanation: Michigan law allows individuals to file private lawsuits as an alternative to or in addition to MDCR complaints. Victims of housing discrimination can pursue court action for damages and injunctive relief.

questions.question_text: A Michigan landlord who owns 12 rental units across the state refuses an otherwise qualified applicant because her rent would be paid partly with a Section 8 housing choice voucher. Is this lawful?
questions.correct_answer: No, source of income is protected for rentals
distractors: Yes, landlords may decline subsidized tenants
Yes, because vouchers are not earned income
Only if the voucher covers the entire rent
questions.explanation: Effective April 2, 2025, 2024 PA 180 added MCL 37.2502(3) to the Elliott-Larsen Civil Rights Act: a landlord may not deny or terminate a tenancy of an otherwise eligible tenant based on source of income, which includes housing choice vouchers, public assistance, Social Security, and veterans' benefits (MCL 554.601). Landlords with fewer than 5 rental units statewide are exempt, but this landlord owns 12. A voucher or subsidy must also be counted when applying any minimum-income screening criteria.
